coredata.dc:title: Comparative study on corrosion inhibition of N doped and N,S codoped carbon dots for carbon steel in strong acidic solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Siming
Cui, Mingjun
Chen, Xinyu
Mei, Shixiang
Qiang, Yujie
coredata.dc:description: Carbon steel is the most widely used engineering material, and its corrosion is one of the main areas of concern in many industries. The most practical approach to control this problem is to use corrosion inhibitors. Currently, because of their good water solubility, excellent chemical stability, low cost and nontoxic features, carbon dots (CDs), especially heteroatom-doped CDs, have been developed as green corrosion inhibitors, but the corrosion inhibition efficiency and underlying mechanisms of single- or dual-element doping have not yet been accurately compared and analyzed. Inspired by this, eco-friendly nitrogen-doped and nitrogen, sulfur codoped CDs (N-CDs and N,S-CDs) are prepared via a one-step hydrothermal process, and a comparative study on their inhibition performance for carbon steel corrosion in strong acidic solution is performed. The results show that both N-CDs and N,S-CDs can restrain the corrosion of carbon steel, and their inhibition efficiency increases with increasing concentration and immersion time, reaching approximately 87.9% (N-CDs) and 96.4% (N,S-CDs) at 200 ppm after 1 h of immersion. Molecular dynamics simulation indicates that the strong interaction ability between N,S-CDs and the Fe substrate leads to higher corrosion inhibition performance than the single N doping case, benefiting from the multi-anchor adsorption of N,S-CDs on carbon steel in a strong acidic solution. Therefore, the facile preparation, eco-friendliness and high corrosion inhibition performance of N,S-CDs will provide a new approach for designing highly efficient carbon dots and broadening the application of carbon dots in the corrosion field.

head.ce:title: Comparative study on corrosion inhibition of N doped and N,S codoped carbon dots for carbon steel in strong acidic solution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon steel is the most widely used engineering material, and its corrosion is one of the main areas of concern in many industries. The most practical approach to control this problem is to use corrosion inhibitors. Currently, because of their good water solubility, excellent chemical stability, low cost and nontoxic features, carbon dots (CDs), especially heteroatom-doped CDs, have been developed as green corrosion inhibitors, but the corrosion inhibition efficiency and underlying mechanisms of single- or dual-element doping have not yet been accurately compared and analyzed. Inspired by this, eco-friendly nitrogen-doped and nitrogen, sulfur codoped CDs (N-CDs and N,S-CDs) are prepared via a one-step hydrothermal process, and a comparative study on their inhibition performance for carbon steel corrosion in strong acidic solution is performed. The results show that both N-CDs and N,S-CDs can restrain the corrosion of carbon steel, and their inhibition efficiency increases with increasing concentration and immersion time, reaching approximately 87.9% (N-CDs) and 96.4% (N,S-CDs) at 200 ppm after 1 h of immersion. Molecular dynamics simulation indicates that the strong interaction ability between N,S-CDs and the Fe substrate leads to higher corrosion inhibition performance than the single N doping case, benefiting from the multi-anchor adsorption of N,S-CDs on carbon steel in a strong acidic solution. Therefore, the facile preparation, eco-friendliness and high corrosion inhibition performance of N,S-CDs will provide a new approach for designing highly efficient carbon dots and broadening the application of carbon dots in the corrosion field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon steel is universally adapted in various industrial applications (e.g., pipelines, constructions, bridges, railways, vehicles, ships and various machinery manufacturing) because of its superior mechanical properties and cost-effectiveness [1–5]. However, carbon steel is susceptible to corrosion during the acid pickling and oil well acidization processes that are always carried out to ensure the normal operation and production of industrial processes. To protect carbon steel from undesirable corrosion during industrial processes, adding corrosion inhibitors to a corrosive medium is considered an economical and useful means. Generally, traditional corrosion inhibitors are compounds containing electronegative heteroatoms (i.e., nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus) and unsaturated bonds that can coordinate with the metal atoms to form adsorbed layers on metal surfaces, thus showing a corrosion inhibiting effect on metals [6–10]. To date, a large number of inorganic and organic corrosion inhibitors have been studied, but most of them are toxic and hazardous to the environment, violating the current concept of green development. Hence, it is highly desirable to develop green, cost-effective and highly efficient corrosion inhibitors.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of zero-dimensional carbon-dominated nanomaterial, have received considerable attention in the field of corrosion protection in recent years owing to their distinct properties, such as facile and easy preparation, low cost and nontoxicity, good water solubility, and excellent chemical stability [11–16]. Regarding metal corrosion, CDs can act as anticorrosive nanofillers to enhance the anticorrosion performance of polymers by taking advantage of the interfacial interaction between neighboring CDs or between CDs and polymer chains as well as the superior oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) performance of CDs [14]. In addition, inspired by the structure of traditional corrosion inhibitors, it is theoretically possible that heteroatom-doped CDs have high potential as green corrosion inhibitors to suppress metal corrosion by electrostatic interactions or forming coordinated bonds between heteroatoms and metal atoms. Until now, heteroatom-doped CDs have been successfully prepared via a bottom-up method (e.g., hydrothermal, solvothermal, and microwave-assisted hydrothermal/solvothermal methods) by adding small heteroatom-containing molecules into the synthesis systems [17–22], in which nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) have become very popular due to abundant nitrogen-containing precursors and the matched size with C atoms [11]. As previously reported, N doping leads to the formation of an N-type structure (pyridinic N and pyrrolic N) in CDs [23,24], in which pyrrolic N can coordinate with metals to promote the adsorption of CDs. Motivated by this, an attempt for N-CDs prepared by the hydothermal method using nitrogen-containing precursors (aminosalicylic acid, phenylenediamine) was reported as a green corrosion inhibitor, and the results showed that N-CDs could effectively inhibit the strong acidic corrosion of carbon steel by Langmuir adsorption and the corresponding inhibition efficiency was up to 90% [25,26]. Subsequently, large amounts of N-CDs prepared with various nitrogenous precursors and synthesis methods were successively used as effective corrosion inhibitors for metallic materials in corrosive solutions [27–32]. In addition to single N doping, codoping of multiple heteroatoms into CDs has been further adopted to synergistically tailor the corrosion inhibition performance of CDs. Cen et al. found that nitrogen, sulfur codoped CDs (N,S-CDs) synthesized by utilizing aminosalicylic acid and thiourea as precursors were effective for the corrosion inhibition of carbon steel in CO2-saturated 3.5% NaCl solution, and its inhibition efficiency determined by the weight loss method was up to 93% at 50 mg/L [33]. Then, novel N,S-CDs with high contents of N (17%) and S (19%) were prepared via a solvothermal approach using o-phenylenediamine and thiourea as precursors. The inhibition efficiency of this type of N,S-CDs on the copper substrate reached 98.5% at a concentration of 50 mg/L in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution [34]. Furthermore, the corrosion inhibition effect of N,S-CDs on aluminum alloy [35], magnesium alloy [36], and mild steel [15,16,37–39] in specific corrosive medium was also investigated to study their potential applications. Although single doping or codoping of heteroatoms is beneficial for preparing highly efficient corrosion inhibitors, few studies have focused on the contribution of single N doping and codoping of N and S in CDs to the corrosion inhibition of metals owing to the discrepancy in pristine precursors, reaction conditions, and evaluation methods. Thus, it is essential to conduct a comparative study on the corrosion inhibition of N-CDs and N,S-CDs to better understand their underlying corrosion inhibition mechanisms.

Herein, we synthesize N-CDs and N,S-CDs via a hydrothermal method with salicylic acid as the carbon source and urea and thiourea as dopants, respectively. Their inhibition effect on the corrosion behavior of Q235 carbon steel immersed in hydrochloric acid (HCl) solution is comparatively investigated by combining electrochemical measurements, weight loss method, and surface analysis using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM), Raman mapping and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). We found that codoping of N and S atoms could yield more pronounced corrosion inhibition and higher inhibition efficiency than single N doping. Furthermore, the inhibiting mechanisms are proposed in light of theoretical calculations and experimental results. This comparative study demonstrates the effectiveness of codoping of N and S atoms in CDs toward high corrosion inhibition performance, and will provide a useful strategy for designing novel green corrosion inhibitors.

Salicylic acid (SA, AR, 99.5%), urea (AR, 99%) and thiourea (TU, AR, 99%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, AR, 36.0–38.0%), acetone (AR, ≥99.5%) and ethanol (AR, 99.7%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Carbon steel substrates with sizes of 30 mm × 20 mm × 3 mm and 10 mm × 10 mm × 1 mm were purchased by Sheng Xin Technology Co., Ltd. Carbon steel substrates were cleaned with acetone and ethanol for 20 min to remove the surface oil contaminants before they were used. Deionized water was homemade in laboratory.

The preparation of N-CDs and N,S-CDs was performed via a typical hydrothermal method. Salicylic acid (SA) and thiourea (TU) or urea (molar ratio is 1:1) were first dissolved into deionized water, and then the mixtures were transferred into Teflon (PTFE) autoclaves, followed by a heating process (200 °C for 4 h (h)). After cooling to room temperature, the obtained dark solutions were transferred into dialysis bags (500 Da) for 24 h of dialysis to remove the unreacted molecules. The final products were collected by removing the solvents with rotary evaporation and dried in a vacuum oven for 12 h, in which N-CDs and N,S-CDs existed in the form of solid powder and colloidal solid, respectively. For comparison, pure CDs were also prepared via the same method using salicylic acid as a precursor.

The micromorphology and size of the specimens were examined with transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200x, ThemoFisher). The chemical structure of the specimens was characterized with Micro-FTIR (Cary660+620, Agilent) with the wavenumbers ranging from 500–4000 cm−1. Raman spectra of specimens were obtained via a confocal microscope Raman spectrometer (Renishaw inVia Reflex) with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The UV–vis spectra were recorded by a Lambda 950 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The elemental composition and bonding state of the specimens were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS ULTRA DLD, England).

The corrosion inhibition performance of the N-CDs and N,S-CDs was evaluated by electrochemical measurements and the weight loss method. The electrochemical measurements were performed on an electrochemistry station (CHI-760E), where a classic three-electrode system was equipped, including a working electrode (carbon steel substrates with 1 cm2 of exposed area), a reference electrode (saturated calomel electrode, SCE) and a counter electrode (Pt electrode with 4 cm2 of area). The corrosive medium was 1 M HCl solution with and without different concentrations of N-CDs (N,S-CDs), in which 1 M HCl solution was obtained by diluting the concentrated hydrochloric acid, and the corrosive medium with 25, 50, 100 and 200 ppm of N-CDs (N,S-CDs) was prepared by adding 2.5, 5, 10 and 20 mg of N-CDs (N,S-CDs) into 100 mL of 1 M HCl solution, respectively. When the corrosive medium was added to the vessel, an open circuit potential (OCP) test was initially carried out for 1 h to ensure that the system was in a steady state, and subsequently, an electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test was recorded at the OCP with an amplitude of 10 mV and a frequency range from 100 kHz to 10 mHz. Additionally, a potentiodynamic polarization test was recorded at a potential range of ±250 mV versus OCP value with a 0.5 mV/s of scanning rate. The weight loss test was carried out by immersing the carbon steel substrates (10 mm × 10 mm × 1 mm) in various corrosive solutions and recording the mass variation of steel substrates at different time intervals. To ensure the reproducibility of the results, the specimens were weighed at least three times with an analytical balance (METTLER TOLEDO, ME104E) during the test.

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation was performed to investigate the adsorption behavior of N-CDs and N,S-CDs on Fe (110) with the Forcite module because Fe (110) is a thermodynamically stable basal plane [40]. A simulation box (24.3 × 17.2 × 81.0 Å) with a 40 Å vacuum layer, 6 layers of Fe (110), 1 inhibitor, 300 H2O, and 5 HCl was created by utilizing a COMPASS force field at 298 K. During the simulation, the position of the Fe layer was immobilized, and the NVT ensemble with 300 ps of simulation time and 1 fs of time step was applied.

After the weight loss test, the surface morphology of the specimens was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, EVO18, ZEISS) and laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSM 700, Zeiss, Germany). Raman mapping on a confocal microscope Raman spectrometer (Renishaw inVia Reflex, Renishaw, UK) was used to check the distribution of adsorbed N-CDs and N,S-CDs on the steel surface, in which the excitation wavelength was 532 nm and the area of the selected region was 30 μm × 30 μm. To avoid carbonization caused by heating effects, the power of the excitation laser was set to 1 mW. The final data were processed with WiRE 4.1 software. The bonding state of the corroded steel surface was further investigated by XPS.

The preparation of N-CDs and N,S-CDs can be accomplished by a one-step hydrothermal process using SA as the carbon source, urea as the N dopant and TU as the N and S dopants at 200 °C for 4 h (Fig. 1 a). Both N-CDs and N,S-CDs can be homogeneously dispersed in ethanol, producing a brown solution under sunlight and a bright fluorescence solution under UV (365 nm). TEM was used to characterize the morphology and size of the as-prepared N-CDs and N,S-CDs. As revealed in Fig. 1b and d, both N-CDs and N,S-CDs are torispherical and distributed uniformly without aggregation. HRTEM images from one selected particle of N-CDs (the inset of Fig. 1b) and N,S-CDs (the inset of Fig. 1d) show lattice spacing distances of approximately 2.0 and 2.2 Å, respectively, which are assigned to the in-plane lattice spacing of graphite (100 facet) [41]. The average particle sizes of the N-CDs and N,S-CDs, calculated from 100 particles, are approximately 3.50 nm (Fig. 1c) and 2.85 nm (Fig. 1e), respectively.

The surface functional groups of the specimens were investigated with micro-FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1f, SA possesses a variety of absorption peaks for the stretching vibrations of –OH (3228 cm−1), CO (1656 cm−1) and the carbon skeleton of the benzene ring (1606, 1441 cm−1). In the spectrum of urea, the adsorption peaks at 3000 ∼ 3500 cm−1 and 1584 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching and bending vibrations of NH in amine groups, respectively. The adsorption peaks at 1678 and 1457 cm−1 are attributed to the CO and CN bonds in the amide group [34,42]. Unlike urea, the characteristic adsorption peaks for TU are located at 1406 and 1082 cm−1, assigned to N–CS [34,43]. After the hydrothermal process of SA with urea or TU, N-CDs and N,S-CDs are formed owing to the polyreaction between SA and urea/TU. For the FTIR spectrum of N-CDs, the adsorption peaks at 3390 ∼ 3290 cm−1, 1589, and 1462 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of –NH (–OH), CC and CN, respectively. In addition to the similar adsorption peaks with N-CDs, a special adsorption peak at 2056 cm−1 can be observed for the N,S-CDs sample, suggesting the formation of NC=S and NC=O after the hydrothermal process [33,37]. Note that the disappearance of carboxyl adsorption peaks and the formation of amide groups in N-CDs and N,S-CDs also verify the reaction between SA and urea/TU. The above results indicate the successful doping of N atoms into N-CDs and codoping of N and S atoms into N,S-CDs. The Raman spectra for N-CDs and N,S-CDs were featured two broad bands at 1325 cm−1 (D band) and 1613 cm−1 (G band) that were ascribed to the sp3 hybridized carbon atoms and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the 2D hexagonal lattice, and the intensity ratio between the D band and G band (ID/IG) was 0.47 and 0.75 for the N-CDs and N,S-CDs, respectively (Fig. 1g). Generally, a higher ID/IG value indicates a high defect degree of graphite carbon [44]. Hence, N,S-CDs may have a higher defect degree than N-CDs. Fig. 1h exhibits the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs, N,S-CDs and the corresponding precursors. Compared with pristine precursors (SA, urea and TU), the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs and N,S-CDs show two characteristic peaks centered at 206 (203) and 285 nm that are attributed to π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains and n-π* of CO, respectively [33,45].

XPS was also employed to determine the elemental composition and bonding configurations of N and S atoms in N-CDs and N,S-CDs. The full-scan XPS spectra for N-CDs and N,S-CDs in Fig. S1 confirm the codoping of N and S in CDs, and the corresponding elemental compositions are summarized in Table S1. In addition, the high-resolution XPS spectra for different elements in N-CDs and N,S-CDs are also analyzed to determine the bonding states of different atoms. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s can be divided into three typical peaks centered at 284.6 (CC/CC), 285.8 (CO, CN, CS) and 288.3 eV (CO, CS) [21,33]. The O 1s spectra are fitted into two components, representing the CO (532.7 eV) and CO (530.8 eV) bonds (Fig. 2b). In the case of the high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s, the peaks at 399.3 and 400.1 eV were related to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N, respectively, confirming the formation of CN bonds in CDs (Fig. 2c) [42,46]. For the S 2p spectrum of N,S-CDs, two peaks at 163.9 and 169.3 eV belong to CS/CS and –CS(O)x-C, respectively (Fig. 2d) [47]. The aforementioned results demonstrate that N-CDs and N,S-CDs have been successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion inhibition performance and mechanisms of N-CDs and N,S-CDs

ce:section.ce:para:
EIS test following with OCP test was conducted to evaluate the corrosion inhibition efficiency of N-CDs and N,S-CDs for carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution. During static immersion, the OCP shifts slightly toward a positive value for N-CDs and a negative value for N,S-CDs with increasing concentration of corrosion inhibitors (Fig. S2). Fig. 3 a-f shows the EIS results of carbon steel after 1 h of immersion in 1 M HCl with and without N-CDs and N,S-CDs. All Nyquist plots with N-CDs consist of a depressed capacitive loop at high-medium frequencies and an inductive loop at low frequencies caused by the relaxation process such as
C l
a d s
-
and
H
a d s
+
on the metal substrate or the adsorption and desorption of inhibitors (Fig. 3a) [25,48]. In the presence of N,S-CDs (Fig. 3d), the Nyquist plots are similar to those with N-CDs when the concentration of N,S-CDs is lower than 50 ppm. The existence of inductive loop suggests that the adsorption of N-CDs (25–200 ppm) and N,S-CDs (25–50 ppm) during 1 h of immersion cannot block all active sites on the steel surface, and the dissolution of metal still occurs. When the concentration of N,S-CDs rises to 100 ppm or higher, the Nyquist plots are composed of the depressed capacitive loop at high-medium frequencies and the Warburg impedance at low frequencies corresponding to the diffusion process of the corrosive medium. The disappearance of the inductive loop indicates that the formation of the adsorbed N,S-CDs film can effectively hinder the active dissolution of steel by inhibiting the transmission of corrosive species. Moreover, the enlargement in the diameter of the capacitive loop with increasing concentrations of N-CDs and N,S-CDs indicates that the charge transfer process (i.e., metal corrosion) is progressively inhibited owing to the adsorption of N-CDs and N,S-CDs. Correspondingly, the impedance modulus at the lowest frequency (Z f
=0.01Hz) in the Bode-impedance modulus plots (Fig. 3b and e) varies from 27 Ω cm2 (0 ppm) to 182 Ω cm2 (N-CDs, 200 ppm) and 468 Ω cm2 (N,S-CDs, 200 ppm). The maximum phase angle in Bode-phase angle plots (Fig. 3c and f) also increases to a higher value with increasing concentrations of N-CDs and N,S-CDs. However, for pure CDs, there is no corrosion inhibition effect on the carbon steel in acidic solution even at 200 ppm (Fig. S3).

The equivalent circuits in Fig. 3g are proposed to better analyze the EIS results. For the carbon steel immersed in blank HCl solution, the equivalent circuit (I) composed of the solution resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct), double-layer capacitance (CPEdl) and inductive resistance (L)/inductance (RL) is used to fit the EIS results. In the presence of N-CDs and N,S-CDs at low concentrations, the equivalent circuit (II) is used to fit their EIS data owing to the adsorption of inhibitors, in which Rs, Rct, CPEdl, RL and L have the same physical meaning as those in the equivalent circuit (I), and CPEf and Rf correspond to the capacitance and resistance of adsorbed films. The equivalent circuit (III) consisting of Rs, Rct, CPEdl, CPEf, Rf and Warburg impedance (W) is more applicable for steel immersed in strong acidic solution with N,S-CDs at high concentrations. In the above equivalent circuits, a constant phase element (CPE) is used instead of an ideal capacitor (C) owing to the roughness and inhomogeneity of the steel surface, which can be expressed as follows [49,50]:

The obtained fitting data are plotted as solid lines in Fig. 3a-f, and the corresponding fitting electrochemical parameters are summarized in Table S2. Note that the fitting lines are extremely consistent with the experimental data with a goodness of fit (chi-squared) within 10-4, indicating the availability of equivalent circuits. As shown in Fig. 3h, the Rct value increases with increasing concentrations of N-CDs and N,S-CDs, indicating that the adsorption of inhibitors on the metal surface suppresses the charge transfer process of metal dissolution. In addition, one can see that the Rct value of N,S-CDs is larger than that of N-CDs at the same concentration, e.g., the maximum Rct value of N,S-CDs at 200 ppm is 436 Ω cm2, which is more than twice as large as that of N-CDs (175 Ω cm2). Meanwhile, the adsorption of inhibitors on the metal surface can replace water with a higher dielectric constant, resulting in a significant decrease in the Cdl value with increasing inhibitor concentration (Fig. 3i). The Rf value varies slightly with increasing concentrations of N-CDs and N,S-CDs within 1 h of immersion while it is higher for N,S-CDs than for N-CDs (Fig. 3j). According to the above EIS results, it can be concluded that N-CDs and N,S-CDs can effectively inhibit metal corrosion in strong acidic solution, but the codoping of N and S atoms outperforms single N doping.

A potentiodynamic polarization test was also performed to investigate the corrosion behavior of carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution with and without N-CDs and N,S-CDs (Fig. 4 a and b). Electrochemical parameters, including corrosion potential (Ecorr ), corrosion current density (i corr), anodic slope (βa ) and cathodic slope (βc ), obtained through the Tafel extrapolation of potentiodynamic polarization plots and the surface coverage

correspond to the corrosion current density in the absence and presence of corrosion inhibitors, respectively. As reported by Kokalj et al., the inhibition efficiency is highly nonlinear; that is, a large increase in the η value (η below approximately 50%) indicates a small reduction in corrosion, while a small increase in the η value (η higher than 90%) suggests a significant reduction in corrosion [54]. In this study, the inhibition efficiency (η) is simplified as the molecular surface coverage (θ), consistent with previous reports. With the increase in the concentration of N-CDs and N,S-CDs, a significant drop in the cathodic corrosion current density and a negative shift in the Ecorr value can be observed for all samples, indicating the dominant cathodic inhibition of inhibitors; that is, the transfer of oxygen from solution to the cathodic sites of the steel substrate is extremely suppressed [55]. Besides, the displacement of Ecorr in the presence of inhibitors is less than 85 mV (Table 1), suggesting that both N-CDs and N,S-CDs are mixed-type inhibitors [56]. When the concentration increases to 200 ppm, the i corr values of the N-CDs and N,S-CDs decrease to 94.5 and 31.1 μA cm−2, and the corresponding inhibition efficiencies are as high as 87.9% and 96.4%, respectively.

To further understand the underlying adsorption isotherms of N-CDs and N,S-CDs on the surface of carbon steel, we used different adsorption isotherm models including Langmuir, Temkin, El-Awady, Flory-Huggins and Freundlich to fit the corresponding results obtained from the potentiodynamic polarization plots. By comparing the fitting results and corresponding linear regression coefficients (R2) based on various adsorption isotherm models (Fig. S4, Table S3, Fig. 4c and d) [57], the Langmuir isotherm with an R2 value close to 1 is more suitable for studying the interaction between N-CDs (N,S-CDs) and the steel surface, which is defined with the following equation [58,59].
(6)

(7)

where R and T represent the molar gas constant and absolute temperature, respectively. As indicated in Fig. 4c and d, the

plots exhibit a linear relationship, and the

Therefore, both N-CDs and N,S-CDs can spontaneously adsorb on the carbon steel surface by chemisorption (transfer and sharing of charge from inhibitor to metal surface) and physisorption (electrostatic and van der Waals interactions) to inhibit the occurrence of corrosion reactions. By comparison, the
Δ G
ads
0
value of N,S-CDs is lower than that of N-CDs, indicating that N,S-CDs have a stronger adsorption capacity and better corrosion inhibition performance.

To provide an in-depth understanding of the adsorption behavior of N-CDs and N,S-CDs on steel surfaces, we utilized MD simulations to investigate the interactions between inhibitors and steel substrates. According to the XPS results, the N atoms exist in the form of pyrrolic nitrogen and pyridinic nitrogen in N-CDs and N,S-CDs, and S atoms are in the form of CS in N,S-CDs. Based on this, the two simplified chemical structures in Fig. 5 a and b are used to represent N-CDs and N,S-CDs, respectively. The stable adsorption configurations of N-CDs and N,S-CDs on Fe (110) are presented in Fig. 5c and d. Regardless of the initial adsorption configuration, N-CDs and N,S-CDs will eventually adsorb on the Fe (110) surface in a parallel state, where the lone electrons of N and S atoms are transferred to the vacant orbital of the Fe atom to form coordination bonds. In this parallel state, N-CDs and N,S-CDs can adsorb on the carbon steel surface with a maximum coverage area, thereby achieving effective corrosion inhibition of carbon steel in a strong acidic solution. Additionally, the binding energy between the adsorbed inhibitor molecules and the metal surface is closely related to the corrosion inhibition performance [62,63]. The calculated binding energies of N-CDs and N,S-CDs on the Fe (110) surface are 165.8 kcal mol−1 and 174.3 kcal mol−1, respectively, indicating a higher interaction between N,S-CDs and Fe (110) than N-CDs [64], which agrees well with the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the growth of adsorption films on steel surface
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the adsorption process of inhibitors, static weight loss measurements were used to determine the variation in the corrosion rates of carbon steel in strong acidic solution with and without N-CDs and N,S-CDs, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency of these two inhibitors. The corrosion rate (
V
corr
) and corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) can be defined by the following equations:
(8)

(9)

× 100 %
where m0 and mt represent the mass of steel before and after t h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution, respectively, and
V
corr
0
and
V
corr
are assigned to the corrosion rate of steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution with and without inhibitors, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 6 a and b, with the addition of N-CDs and N,S-CDs, the Vcorr value shows a significant drop compared with that in blank acid solution, and decreases progressively with the increase in inhibitor concentration and the extension of immersion time while the inhibition efficiency (η) exhibits the opposite trend. After 24 h of immersion, the Vcorr and η values reach a relatively stable value, indicating that the adsorption of inhibitors reaches an equilibrium status at this time. Notably, the inhibition efficiency for N,S-CDs is higher than that of N-CDs at the same concentration and immersion time, further proving the effectiveness of the codoping of N and S in CDs. The corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency obtained at 200 ppm N-CDs and N,S-CDs for 24 h of immersion are 0.46 g m-2h−1, 95.4% and 0.17 g m-2h−1, 98.4%, respectively.

The growth of the adsorption film with 200 ppm N-CDs and N,S-CDs was further investigated by EIS method, and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 6c-d and Fig. S5. In the presence of 200 ppm N-CDs and N,S-CDs, enlarged capacitive loops and an increased impedance modulus can be observed as the immersion time extends from 1 to 24 h, indicating that the continuous adsorption of inhibitors on the steel surface effectively suppresses the penetration of corrosive species (Fig. 6c-d, Fig. S5). In addition, the diameter of capacitive loops and Z f
=0.01Hz values for N,S-CDs are larger than those of N-CDs at the same immersion time. For instance, the Z f
=0.01Hz value in the presence of N,S-CDs varies from 468 Ω cm2 at 1 h to 1445 Ω cm2 at 24 h, which is nearly twice as large as that in the presence of N-CDs (182 Ω cm2 at 1 h to 832 Ω cm2 at 24 h), suggesting the superior corrosion inhibition performance of N,S-CDs (Fig. 6e). To quantitatively analyze the adsorption of inhibitors with immersion time, the EIS data were further fitted with equivalent circuits. For the carbon steel within 6 h of immersion in the presence of N-CDs, the equivalent circuit (II) in Fig. 3g is still appropriately used to fit the EIS results. As the immersion time is further prolonged (12 and 24 h), the continuous adsorption of N-CDs on steel surface, effectively inhibits the dissolution of steel, thus leading to the disappearance of the inductive loop in the Nyquist plots. At this time, the penetration of corrosive species along the adsorption film becomes obvious, so a diffusive feature can be observed at low frequencies. Consequently, the equivalent circuit (III) in Fig. 3g is used to fit the EIS results for this duration. In the presence of 200 ppm N,S-CDs, the equivalent circuit (III) in Fig. 3g is always appropriate to fit the results within 24 h of immersion, as indicated by the good agreement between the fitting and experimental results in Fig. 6c and d. The corresponding fitting parameters are summarized in Table S4 and the Rf values are plotted in Fig. 6f. During 24 h of immersion, the Rf value in the presence of 200 ppm N-CDs increases gradually from 15.3 to 141.0 Ω cm2 while it shows a dramatic increase from 31.4 to 490.0 Ω cm2 in the presence of 200 ppm N,S-CDs. It is worth noting that the Rf value in the presence of N,S-CDs reaches a high value within a shorter immersion time (6 h), suggesting that N,S-CDs are easier to adsorb on the steel surface than N-CDs, consistent with the calculated
Δ G
a d s
0
results.

The corrosion morphology of carbon steel after 60 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution with and without 200 ppm N-CDs and N,S-CDs was analyzed by SEM. After 60 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution, the surface of carbon steel is severely corroded, and numerous corrosion products are formed compared with the pristine ones (Fig. S6 and Fig. 7 a). In stark contrast, the corrosion degree of the steel surface is reduced dramatically with increasing concentrations of N-CDs and N,S-CDs (Fig. S7 and Fig. 7b-c), and polishing scratches are still clearly visible in the presence of 200 ppm N,S-CDs. Afterward, the distribution state of N-CDs and N,S-CDs on the steel surface was characterized by Raman mapping measurement, and the testing region was selected randomly from Fig. 7d-f. Fig. 7h-j shows the distribution of the integrated intensity of G peak (∼1610 cm−1) assigned to the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in the N-CDs and N,S-CDs. Almost no signal is detected for carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution, while an obvious Raman peak of the G band can be observed after 60 h of immersion in 1 M HCl with 200 ppm N-CDs and N,S-CDs, as confirmed by the increased Raman intensity (Fig. 7h-j). More importantly, the whole surface of the sample with the addition of N,S-CDs shows a stronger signal of the G peak than N-CDs, indicating that a dense and well-covered adsorption layer is formed on the steel surface in the presence of N,S-CDs.

XPS was employed to investigate the elemental composition and bonding state of the steel surface after 60 h of immersion in 1 M HCl solution with and without 200 ppm N-CDs and N,S-CDs. The XPS data for steel immersed in blank HCl solution are the same as those in our previous investigation owing to the same testing conditions [65]. As shown in Fig. 8 a, taking the blank sample as a reference, the significant enhancement of the C 1s peak intensity and the appearance of N and S signals demonstrate the adsorption of N-CDs and N,S-CDs on the steel surface. For the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 8b), the deconvoluted peaks at 398.7 and 399.8 eV correspond to Fe−N bond and pyrrolic N, respectively [15,32,66], evidencing the physisorption and chemisorption of N-CDs and N,S-CDs on the steel surface. The S 2p spectrum can be fitted with two peaks centered at 163.7 and 162.1 eV that are assigned to CS/CS and Fe-S [33,67], respectively (Fig. 8c), strongly proving the chemisorption of N,S-CDs. For the Fe 2p spectra (Fig. 8d), two peaks at approximately 710.0 and 712.0 eV are related to the Fe2O3 and FeOOH species, respectively. Additionally, the percentage of FeOOH is greatly reduced owing to the suppressed cathodic reaction when the N-CDs and N,S-CDs are added into the solution. Another peak at 706.3 eV assigned to metallic Fe can be seen in the Fe 2p spectrum of the sample with the addition of 200 ppm N,S-CDs. The existence of metallic Fe is a direct evidence of mild corrosion of steel surfaces, suggesting the promising application of N,S-CDs as green corrosion inhibitors.

Generally, the dissolution of metal would occur when the bare steel is placed in 1 M HCl solution, and the main mechanism for the anodic dissolution reaction and cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction can be described as follows [68,69]:

With the addition of N-CDs and N,S-CDs, the corrosion of steel is significantly inhibited, and rational corrosion inhibition mechanisms for N-CDs and N,S-CDs in acid solution are proposed in Fig. 9 a-c. First, N-CDs and N,S-CDs with abundant amino groups (evidence in Fig. 1) are easily protonated when immersed into 1 M HCl solution (N-CDs +
H
+
↔
[N-CDs
H
+
], N,S-CDs + H+
↔
[N,S-CDs
H
+
]). In this scenario, the protonated N-CDs or N,S-CDs can be electrostatically adsorbed on the cathodic sites (adsorbed Cl ions) of the steel surface, which would reduce the quantity of hydrogen ions going to participate in cathodic hydrogen evolution, thus inhibiting the dissolution of steel (Fig. 9a). On the other hand, N-CDs and N,S-CDs can directly anchor onto the steel surface via the coordination bond that is formed by the electron transfer from the pyrrolic nitrogen atoms in N-CDs and N,S-CDs to the vacant d orbitals of Fe atoms (i.e., forming Fe-N bonds), as schematically shown in Fig. 9b. In addition to Fe-N bonds, the S atoms in the CS/CS bond of N,S-CDs can also coordinate with Fe atoms to form Fe-S bonds (Fig. 9c). With the synergistic assistance of N and S atoms, the adsorption efficiency of N,S-CDs on the metal surface is much better than that of single N doping. Combining the aforementioned corrosion inhibition mechanisms, N,S-CDs show an inhibition efficiency up to 98.4 % at 200 ppm within 24 h of immersion, which outperform many CDs-based materials and some traditional corrosion inhibitors (Fig. 9d). Details (substrate, corrosive medium, temperature, concentration of inhibitors, immersion time and inhibition efficiency) on the inhibitors involved in Fig. 9d and CDs used for other corrosion inhibition experiments are given in Table S5. As expected, N,S-CDs exhibit multi-anchor synergistically strengthened corrosion inhibition of carbon steel in a strong acidic solution, which will pave the way for developing novel and eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors.

In this study, two kinds of carbon dots (CDs) — nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) and nitrogen and sulfur codoped CDs (N,S-CDs) are synthesized via a green hydrothermal approach, and evaluated as corrosion inhibitors for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution. Electrochemical results show that both N-CDs and N,S-CDs as mixed-type corrosion inhibitors have high corrosion inhibition performance for carbon steel in acidic solution. Especially for N,S-CDs, the inhibition efficiency reaches 96.4% after 1 h of immersion at 200 ppm. Molecular dynamics simulation results reveal that N-CDs and N,S-CDs are adsorbed tightly on the Fe (110) surface with a parallel state, and N,S-CDs show higher binding energy with Fe atoms, which is consistent with the experimental results. Furthermore, by combining the electrochemical and weight loss results with characterization of the corroded surface, we find that N,S-CDs can form a stable adsorption film on the steel surface against erosion from corrosive media with prolonged immersion time. The corresponding inhibition efficiency of N,S-CDs reaches approximately 98.4% after 24 h of immersion, which is higher than our prepared N-CDs, other reported CDs-based materials and some traditional inhibitors [15,28,30,34,37–39,61,68], suggesting that the multi-anchor adsorption of N and S atoms on the metal substrate is critical for the high inhibition performance of N,S-CDs. This study provides thoughts and guidance for designing new carbon dots with high inhibition efficiency toward corrosive environments.
